question: How does the URI pattern simulate Higher-Kinded Types in TypeScript?

options.text: Through a URItoKind<A> registry where string keys map to type constructors, and Kind<F, A> = URItoKind<A>[F]
Through class inheritance and virtual methods
options.explanation: Inheritance is a runtime pattern. HKT simulation works entirely at the type level through indexed access and declaration merging

options.text: Through conditional types and infer
options.explanation: Conditional types and infer are type-level programming tools but not the HKT simulation mechanism. The URI pattern uses an interface registry and indexed access types

options.text: TypeScript natively supports HKT starting from version 5.0
options.explanation: TypeScript has no native HKT support in any version. The URI pattern (defunctionalization) is a workaround popularized by the fp-ts library

question: What does const do in a generic parameter position: function f<const T extends string>(x: T)?

options.text: Forces TypeScript to infer the narrowest (literal) type for T
Makes T immutable (readonly)
options.explanation: const in generic position affects type inference, not mutability. For arrays it gives readonly, but the main purpose is preserving literal types instead of widening to string

options.text: Forbids passing variables — only literals allowed
options.explanation: const T doesn't restrict calls — you can still pass variables. It affects how TypeScript infers the type: preferring literal types over widened ones

options.text: Equivalent to as const for the argument
options.explanation: const in generic position is similar to as const but works at the generic inference level. as const applies to values, while const T applies to the type parameter

question: Which statements are true for the "Distributive Object Types" pattern?

options.text: The pattern { [K in keyof T]: F<K, T[K]> }[keyof T] creates a union from a mapped type
Each union member preserves the exact association between key and value type
The result is always an intersection, not a union
options.explanation: Indexing a mapped type via [keyof T] creates a union, not an intersection. For an intersection you would need UnionToIntersection

options.text: This pattern only works with interface, not type alias
options.explanation: The pattern works identically with interface and type alias. It's based on mapped types and indexed access, which don't depend on how the type is declared

question: Why does TypedBuilder's field method return a new TypedBuilder<Schema & Record<K, V>> instead of this?

options.text: So the type progressively accumulates — each field call extends Schema with a new field
Because this cannot be used as a return type
options.explanation: In TypeScript this can be used as a return type. But when returning this, the Schema type doesn't change, and the new field is not added to the type

options.text: For better compiler performance
options.explanation: It's not about performance but type safety. Returning this doesn't add the new field to Schema, so build() would return an incomplete type

options.text: Because TypeScript doesn't support mutation of generic parameters
options.explanation: This is true but incomplete. Precisely because generics can't be mutated, we create a new TypedBuilder with an extended type via intersection

options.text: To block type inference of T from a specific argument position — T is only inferred from other positions
To completely disable type inference
options.explanation: NoInfer doesn't disable inference entirely — it blocks inference only from a specific position. T is still inferred from other arguments

options.text: To speed up compilation by skipping inference
options.explanation: NoInfer doesn't affect compilation speed. Its purpose is to control which argument positions TypeScript uses to infer the type

options.text: To convert infer to never
options.explanation: NoInfer doesn't turn the type into never. It simply prevents that position from being used as a source for inferring type T
